children.text:
Welcome. I am happy to share this moment with you. I am a radio host and a friend of Puerto Rican culture. To everyone, welcome to this eighth Encuentro de Tambores. Created by Norma Salazar, today in July we celebrate a historic event where professionals, students, enthusiasts, and lovers of the culture of Puerto Rico and of bomba come together in a greater gathering of the oldest musical genre in our tradition.
We especially thank the Institute of Puerto Rican Culture and Professor Carlos Luis Cortes of Cataño for welcoming the bomba cultural community to this beautiful city. Cataño offers us an event full of cultural, social, and historical meaning. The formation of you, the people of Cataño, and the African heritage that lives through bomba in Puerto Rico remain present in your streets and in the memory of your town.
May this cultural family continue growing in our community. Norma taught us through her example, so another round of applause for Norma. She proposes the song, she proposes the rhythm, and at this moment we present a bomba maraca — a completely handcrafted maraca.
This maraca will be given to Don Miguel, the dancer. Although he is an elder, the one who stays with his generation and remains active becomes an example for all of us: for the cultural community at every level — officials, students, and lovers of the genre. Don Miguel has shown a deep commitment to this genre of bomba and to the African heritage we celebrate. He has carried it with pride today and every day of the year, throughout his entire life.
That is why we present him with this maraca: because he is an elder among all the bomberos and bomberas that exist in the world. Don Miguel, do you carry the rhythm? Likewise, this maraca will remain for the future generations of his family as a legacy. Today is a very special day. Here we present this piece so that he may keep it in his home as part of the official archive of the Encuentro de Tambores, a gift from the ICP, with the date of the Encuentro de Tambores created by Norma Salazar. Don Miguel Flores, from Cataño, March 18, 2018… so, for your little box, here it is.

“Suena los Tambores” Delegación del Sur, directed by José Archeval
Chorus
Drums, drums
Drums, drums
Drums of more
Verses
The drums sound in the early morning
The drums sound, rise up
The drums sound, (unintelligible)
The drums sound, African rhythms
Let’s go dance!
Rain drums
Drums of the sea
Our culture, which we must defend
Drums, drums, drums of wood
Drums, drums of (my good friend)
The drums sound so we can dance
Rain drums, ay, drums of the sea
Drums that sing
Drums that speak
All made of molasses
Ay, drums, drums that cry
Drums that suffer, when the history of men destroys
Drums that celebrate
Drums, drums of leather and wood
Drums, drums (unintelligible)
Ah!

“Cómo tú pretendes?” 
Intro
Sound my barrels!
The South has arrived.
Chorus
How do you expect me to love you?
How do you expect me to adore you?
If you love me in that way, in that
way, hearts cry.

annotation.text: "“Bomba me está llamando"" Delegación del Sur

Chorus
Bomba is calling me.
Bomba is calling me.
The conga and my bomba, they are ringing out for me!
"Mi negro, cójanlo suave" Delegacion del Sur

Chorus
My man, take it easy… he went too far… the…
My man, take it easy… it stays with me…
My man, take it easy… look at him…

Verses
Angolas
Softly, softly, buleador
Keep going, keep playing
Ay, my man
Ay, I look at him, my heart

children.text: La última canción del repertorio de la Delegación del Sur. Canción no identificada.
La Delegación de Carolina comienza su presentación. Están montando los tambores. Periodo de transición.

annotation.text: Bomba no identificada interpretada por la Delegación de las Carolinas
Con mi abajo, hoya y mula
Ay Maria, llega a la historia
Ay Maria, me da la memoria

children.text: "La negra pancha" Delegacion de las Carolinas

annotation.text: Delegacion del Este. Primer canto de bomba.

children.text: Transición a La Delegacion del Oeste

annotation.text: Somos semillas, somos el fruto de la bomba
Somos las flores, somes retonos, somos capullos
Y mi herencia son los tambores.

children.text: Tercer cancion del Delegación del Oeste

“Me voy pa’ la bomba” Delegación de los Cangrejos
Chorus
I’m going, I’m going to the bomba
I’m going, I’m going to the bomba with the full moon
Verses
To the full moon, I go
I’m going, I’m going to the bomba, Mama, with the full moon

“Santera” Delegación de los Cangrejos
Chorus
Santera, yes santera, don’t cry anymore
Verses
Don’t cry anymore, santera
Ay, santera, yes santera, freedom
Ay, santera, yes santera
Applauses for the delegation del Cangrejos that is directed by Pablo Luis Rivera. Transitioning to the Delegacion del Loiza.

“Los tambores están sonando” Delegación de Loíza
Chorus
The drums are sounding
The drums are calling
Tell me, tell them that in Loíza they are ringing out.
Verses
For the masters of bomba, thanks to the Ayalas

“Quiere mi bomba” Delegación de Loíza
Chorus
From the African and the soul, my bomba desires
From the African and the soul, my bomba desires

annotation.text: I will dance, I will dance
The bomba of Loiza, I will dance.

children.text: Delegación de la Diáspora se entra.

“La tradición que la bomba de verdad” Delegación de la Diáspora
Chorus
It is needed among us
The maroons from times past
To the hearts
The tradition that is the true bomba
Verses
The tradition that never dies in me
The tradition that is the true bomba

tags.tag: La tradicion que la bomba de verdad

annotation.text: "Cuembe pa' lo más corrido" Delegación de la Diáspora

children.text: The batey is opened to the general public. Marien mentions the original founder of the Encuentro de Tambores, Norma Salazar. This section is called the “gran batey."
